2 min read
3 hours ago

--

After setting up my Angular 21 workspace on Day 1, I deliberately avoided jumping straight into coding.
Instead, I wanted to think like a Solution Architect.
One lesson from my previous enterprise projects is that successful applications are designed before they are developed.
The Question
If I were building an Employee Management Portal from scratch, what decisions should be made before writing the first component?
Discussions
During this exercise, I explored several architectural topics:

• Identifying business modules such as Authentication, Employee, Admin, Leave, Payroll and Reports.

• Defining role-based authorization (HR, Manager, Employee).

• Understanding why backend authorization is as important as UI validation.

• Distinguishing Functional Requirements from Non-Functional Requirements.

• Planning for scalability using server-side pagination, filtering and sorting.

• Comparing Git Flow, GitHub Flow and Trunk-Based Development.

• Understanding why architecture reduces merge conflicts by defining clear ownership boundaries.
A Key Learning
One important realization was the difference between reusable UI and reusable business logic.
For example:

• Buttons, dialogs and loaders belong in a Shared framework.

• Employee screens belong to the Employee feature.

• Other modules should reuse Employee APIs and models rather than directly depending on Employee UI components.
This keeps the application loosely coupled and easier to maintain.
Biggest Takeaway
For the first time in a long while, I wasn’t thinking about Angular syntax.
I was thinking about software architecture, scalability and engineering decisions.
That reminded me that building enterprise applications is much more than writing components.
The code should be the result of good design — not the starting point.
What’s Next?
On Day 3, I’ll start translating this architecture into an actual Angular 21 project structure and begin implementing the application.
